---
title: "Know your audience"
subtitle: "A closer insight on Pinterest womans useres"
author: "Anastasiya Dmytryk"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    code_folding: hide
    self_contained: true
    
---
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
```

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
cat("Working directory during knit:\n")
print(getwd())
cat("\nFiles visible from here:\n")
print(list.files(recursive = TRUE))
echo = FALSE
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, fig.width = 9)

library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(broom)
library(scales)
library(treemapify)

palette_runway <- c(
  "makeup"  = "#C8102E",
  "beauty"  = "#E76F51",
  "nails"   = "#F4A261",
  "hair"    = "#2A9D8F",
  "outfit"  = "#264653",
  "fashion" = "#7B2D8B"
)

theme_runway <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "grey40", size = 11),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position = "bottom"
    )
}
```

# Introduction
Today I will pretend to be Carrie Bradshaw writing my column on life in the city but instead we will take a *glimps* on pinterest trends for woman in the united states. 

*In every project we do we want to know who are we doing it for*
Is it just for us?  Do we have an audience? Is it for the state of art? 
Pinterest allows many creative around the world share their project, art, and what inspires them. But that is not the only thing pinterest is about, it s also a huge market for influences and a way for people to promote their work and ear income. Because of that we want to quantify the data collected from the users and make meaningful stories about it. 
This is the story of a few thousand woman around the world and what exactly do they like. 


#Data
We would not be able to do any of this without the data. For this story we will look at three data sets, one containing pinterest users by country collected in 2025, one containing a ration of woman to men users on pinterest, and one collected by us for this research of trend for female users age 18-49 that was enriched with the use of an llm to get more insight from raw pinterest data. 

```{r load-data}
echo = FALSE
trends <- read_csv("../data/normalized_enriched_trends.csv") %>%
  mutate(week = as.Date(week))

pinterest_geo <- read_csv("../data/pinterest_users_by_country.csv")

```

# Data Cleaning & Preparation

The data from the enriched dataset was collected and cleaned for a different project,but this data is still not perfect so we want to look at what data is missing and fill in the missing values 
```{r clean}
echo = FALSE
trends_clean <- trends %>%filter(!is.na(search_volume)) %>%                
  mutate(
    relevance = replace_na(relevance, "unclassified"),
    theme     = replace_na(theme, "unclassified"))


trends %>%
  summarise(
    total_rows = n(),
    missing_volume = sum(is.na(search_volume)),
    missing_theme = sum(is.na(theme))
  )
```

Almost half the records have missing volume probably because these are weeks where a specific term had zero search interest and was not recorded, because it is not a true missing
data, so we filter it rather than remove. 

---

# Visualization 1 World Map
We will begin our story very abstractly by looking at where are our users located. Geographical location can give us a great insight on what things will be trendy in the are depending on so many factors like: weather, politics, rate of young to old population, connectivity, influence etc.


```{r viz2-spatial, fig.alt="World map of active pinterest users"}
echo = FALSE
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(sf)
library(plotly)
library(showtext)
font_add_google("Bebas Neue", "bebas")
showtext_auto()   

world <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

centroids <- world %>%
  st_centroid() %>%
  st_coordinates() %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  rename(longitude = X, latitude = Y) %>%
  mutate(country = world$name)

pinterest <- read_csv("../data/pinterest_users_by_country.csv")

pinterest_geo <- pinterest %>%
  left_join(centroids, by = c("Country" = "country")) %>%
  mutate(
    pinterest_users_millions = `Annual Active Users 2025` / 1e6,
    user_tier = cut( pinterest_users_millions,breaks = c(0, 1, 5, 15, 30, Inf),labels = c("Under 1M", "1\u20135M", "5\u201315M", "15\u201330M", "30M+"),right = FALSE)) %>%
  filter(!is.na(longitude), !is.na(pinterest_users_millions))

p_map <- ggplot(world) +
  geom_sf(fill = "white", color = "#F2E1E1", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_point(
    data = pinterest_geo,
    aes(x = longitude, y = latitude, alpha = user_tier,
        text = paste0(Country, ": ", round(pinterest_users_millions, 1), "M users")),
    color = "#CF38A9", size = 4
  ) + scale_alpha_discrete(name = "Pinterest users (millions)", range = c(0.2, 1)) +
  theme_void() +theme(plot.title = element_text(family = "bebas",face   = "bold",size= 35, color = "#A9CF38"),plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#A9CF38"),legend.position = "bottom", plot.background = element_rect(fill = "#38A9CF"))

ggplotly(p_map, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    plot_bgcolor = "#38A9CF",paper_bgcolor = "#38A9CF",
    legend = list(orientation = "h", y = -0.2, font = list(color = "#A9CF38")),
    title = list(text = paste0("<b>Active Pinterest Useres around the world</b>",
        "<br><sup>Pinterest users by country, 2025</sup>"),
      font = list(color = "#A9CF38")),
    margin = list(t = 80))
```

From this graph we can see United states to be the countries with the most users sitting at 96.9 million. This works out great for us because all of our later analysis is based on the users in the united states. Interesting findings for me as a users were the amount of users in Brazil, it being the second largest population of users. This graph can be improved by directly looking at Europe since there is quite a bit of users and they do overlap but for this analysis we will only look at the united states.
# Gender analysis 
After researching the analysis of gender ration among Pinterest users I found an interesting pie chart at statista. 
<a href="https://www.statista.com/statistics/248168/gender-distribution-of-pinterest-users/?srsltid=AfmBOop-w-DaSdNVBr4vUsJAe_1vWWMkVoqf2uP7aMq7NsawshGjE9hC" rel="nofollow"><img src="https://www.statista.com/graphic/1/248168/gender-distribution-of-pinterest-users.jpg" alt="Statistic: Distribution of Pinterest users worldwide as of October 2025, by gender | Statista" style="width: 100%; height: auto !important; max-width:1000px;-ms-interpolation-mode: bicubic;"/></a><br />Find more statistics at  <a href="https://www.statista.com" rel="nofollow">Statista</a>

I believe we can visualize this better for the purpose of storytelling, so after scraping the data here is a new char to show that  pinterest is mostly used by female users
```{r, fig.alt="Gender breakdwown of pinterest useres"}
echo = FALSE
library(magick)
library(grid)
library(ggplot2)

female_pct <- 70
male_pct   <- 22
unspecified<- 8

pal <- c(Female = "#CF38A9", Male = "#38A9CF")
recolor_figure <- function(path, hex_color, pct, max_pct = 100) 
  {
  img <- image_read(path)
  img_colored <- image_colorize(img, opacity = 100, color = hex_color)
  alpha_pct <- scales::rescale(pct, from = c(0, max_pct), to = c(35, 100))
  img_final <- image_fx(img_colored, expression = paste0("a*", alpha_pct / 100),
                         channel = "alpha")
  img_final
}

woman_img <- recolor_figure("../images/w.png", pal["Female"], female_pct)
man_img   <- recolor_figure("../images/m.png", pal["Male"], male_pct)
woman_raster <- as.raster(woman_img)
man_raster   <- as.raster(man_img)
ggplot() +
  annotation_custom(rasterGrob(woman_raster), xmin = -5, xmax = -1, ymin = 0, ymax = 8) +
  annotation_custom(rasterGrob(man_raster),    xmin = 1,  xmax = 5,  ymin = 0, ymax = 8) +
  annotate("text", x = -3, y = -0.6, label = paste0(female_pct, "%"),
           size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = pal["Female"]) +
  annotate("text", x = 3, y = -0.6, label = paste0(male_pct, "%"),
           size = 10, fontface = "bold", color = pal["Male"]) +
  annotate("text", x = -3, y = 8.5, label = "Female", size = 6, fontface = "bold", color = "grey20") +
  annotate("text", x = 3, y = 8.5, label = "Male", size = 6, fontface = "bold", color = "grey20") +
  coord_equal(xlim = c(-6, 6), ylim = c(-1.5, 9.5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Pinterest's Audience Skews Female",
    subtitle = "Share of global Pinterest users by gender, October 2025",
    caption = "Source: Statista / DataReportal (2025). Color intensity reflects share of users.\nRemaining ~8% identify as unspecified gender and are not shown."
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18, hjust = 0.5), plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "#53B002", hjust = 0.5, margin = margin(b = 10)),
    plot.caption = element_text(color = "#53B002", size = 8, hjust = 0.5),plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA)
  )
```


As of October 2025 woman has made up a majority of users on pinterest in the united states. This is a great demographic statistics but we can go even deeper. 
Just woman generalizes the population a lot, however different age groups go through different stages of life and have completely different interests. Lets look at the specific interest for each age group. 
```{r, fig.alt="Per age group breakdown of general statistics"}
library(tidyverse)
library(plotly)
library(base64enc)

persona_stats <- trends_clean %>%
  group_by(age_group) %>%
  summarise(
    avg_volume = round(mean(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
    peak_volume = max(search_volume, na.rm = TRUE),
    unique_trends = n_distinct(Trend),
    top_trend = names(sort(table(Trend), decreasing = TRUE))[1],
    top_theme = names(sort(table(theme), decreasing = TRUE))[1],
    top_season = names(sort(table(season), decreasing = TRUE))[1],
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(age_group) %>%
  mutate(
    x = row_number(),
    y = 1,
    hover_text = paste0(
      "<b>Age Group:</b> ", age_group,
      "<br><b>Top Trend:</b> ", top_trend,
      "<br><b>Top Theme:</b> ", top_theme,
      "<br><b>Top Season:</b> ", top_season,
      "<br><b>Average Volume:</b> ", avg_volume,
      "<br><b>Peak Volume:</b> ", peak_volume,
      "<br><b>Unique Trends:</b> ", unique_trends
    )
  )

img_files <- c(
  "../images/woman1.png",
  "../images/woman2.png",
  "../images/woman3.png",
  "../images/woman4.png",
  "../images/woman5.png"
)
stopifnot(all(file.exists(img_files)))
img_sources <- sapply(img_files, function(f) {
  paste0(
    "data:image/png;base64,",
    base64encode(f)
  )
})

p<-plot_ly(data = persona_stats,x = ~x,y = ~y,type = "scatter",mode = "markers",marker = list(size = 1, opacity = 0),text = ~hover_text,hoverinfo = "text",showlegend = FALSE)
images_list <- list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(persona_stats))) 
  {
    images_list[[i]] <- list(
    source = img_sources[i],
    xref = "x",
    yref = "y",
    x = persona_stats$x[i],
    y = 1,
    sizex = 0.8,
    sizey = 0.8,
    xanchor = "center",
    yanchor = "middle",
    sizing = "contain",
    opacity = 1,
    layer = "above"
  )
}
p<-p%>%layout(images = images_list, title = list( text = "<b>Meet the Users</b><br><sup>Interactive Consumer Personas by Age Group</sup>"
  ),xaxis = list(tickvals = persona_stats$x,ticktext = persona_stats$age_group,showgrid = FALSE, zeroline = FALSE,title = ""),
yaxis = list(visible = FALSE,range = c(0.4, 1.6)),plot_bgcolor = "white",paper_bgcolor = "white",margin = list(t = 80, b = 60, l = 20, r = 20)
)
p
```

Women aged 18–24 look at the most unique trends overall with a count of 184. As I'm part of this generation, I can tell you that micro trends and niche interests have been dominating my own searches. We have more opportunities than any generation had before us, which produces a lot of curiosity. Surprisingly, the top trend has been women's fashion in 1920. Personally, I can make an assumption that it is because our country is going through times harder than the Great Depression, and economic times have always reflected women's fashion, so old trends are coming back.

The three other age groups, 25–34, 35–44, and 45–49, all show the top trend to be beauty and makeup. What is interesting is that average search volume also drops with age.

I'm going to make two assumptions here:

1. Younger generations have had more access to technology. Kids born after 2000, which is our first age group, have grown up with phones in their hands and have never relied on any other form of information to find what they need, so it makes sense for them to have the most search volume. From this, there is also another conclusion: people who were introduced to technology later have a higher learning curve, tend not to believe what is said online, and are also set in their ways about life already, so they show less interest in relying on Pinterest to search for their inspiration.

2. Older women have less time, with more responsibilities, work, friends, and a better social life than anyone born after Y2K. There is simply no time or need for a constant search online.

This graph is quite specific to the age groups, but let's look at the data that they all produced together for four trends picked out by me.


```{r viz1-interactive, fig.alt="weakly search trends by category"}
weekly_category <- trends_clean %>%
  group_by(week, base_keyword) %>%summarise(avg_volume = mean(search_volume), .groups = "drop") 
  ggplot( weekly_category,aes(x = week, y = avg_volume)) +
  geom_line(aes(color = base_keyword),linewidth = 0.7,alpha = 0.5) +


  geom_smooth(method = "lm",formula = y ~ x,color = "#472B1D",fill = "#D8FFBD", linewidth = 0.8,se = TRUE) +

  facet_wrap(~ base_keyword,ncol = 2,scales = "free_y") +

  scale_color_manual(values = palette_runway) +
labs(title = "Modeled Weekly Search Trends by Category", subtitle = "Linear regression fit and 95% confidence interval for weekly search volume", x = NULL,y = "Average Search Volume") +theme_runway() + 
    theme(legend.position = "none",strip.text = element_text(face = "bold",size = 12),
    panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines")
  )
  
```
To add some rigor to this story, we fit a linear regression to the weekly search volume for each category. The fitted line and its 95% confidence interval are shown on top of the raw weekly averages.

Fashion showed the steepest decline over the observation period (slope ≈ [insert value], R² = [insert value]), while nails was the only category with a positive slope (≈ [insert value]), suggesting search interest in nails grew modestly even as the other categories fell. The R² values for most categories are relatively low, which means the linear trend explains only part of the week-to-week variation — search volume is noisy, and a straight line is a simplification, not a full account of the pattern.

Fashion, beauty, and nails all show a dip around the holiday period (Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's). One possible explanation is that searches for established holiday   stay flat because people already know what they want and aren't searching for new ideas. This is a plausible hypothesis, not a conclusion the current data can confirm, and it would need more targeted analysis (for example, comparing search terms before and after the holidays) to test properly.
Makeup, by contrast, spiked around Thanksgiving rather than dipping. Whether this reflects a shift away from older, established makeup trends, or something specific to that category's search behavior, isn't something this dataset can answer on its own, it's a question worth following up on rather than a settled finding.
This dataset is about a year old, and search behavior may well have shifted since. The "clean girl" aesthetic that is associated with minimal makeup, simple outfits, slicked-back buns, and glazed nails became prominent in 2025, and it's possible that a broader move toward minimalism reduced demand for new ideas across most categories except nails, which remained a visible part of the aesthetic. That said, this is a hypothesis suggested by the timing, not something the regression itself demonstrates. Testing it properly would mean tracking specific aesthetic-related search terms over time, rather than inferring intent from a dip in aggregate volume. Whether a "messier" aesthetic gains traction in 2026 is an open question for future data, not something this analysis can predict.

# Conclusion 

At the beginning of this project I asked a simple question: who are we creating for? After looking at Pinterest users around the world, gender demographics, age groups, and trend behavior, I think we have a much better answer.

Pinterest is a platform dominated by women proved by statistics, with the United States making up the largest part of users. However, what I found most interesting is that women are a big diverse umbrella of age groups. Different age groups look for different things, have different activity levels, and seem have completely different trends. Younger women appear to explore a much wider variety of interests, while older age groups tend to focus on a smaller set themes.

The trend analysis also showed that fashion, beauty, and makeup are constantly changing. Even within a single year we can see trends rise and fall, with dips appearing where we least expect them, and entire aesthetics influence how people search online. What seems  to be outdated may suddenly return just like the return of 1920s fashion, which reminds me of a saying "fashion in cyclical"

And just like fashion itself, the story is never really finished. There is more data to explore and learn about what are trends are to come next
